links.rationale: Covers the 'ask' half of Ohio's ask-and-answer questioning routine; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, so it also serves RI.2.1.
Covers the 'answer from the text' half — using text evidence to support a response about key details; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.2.1.
Both require determining the lesson/theme of a literary work from text evidence; TX literary-elements SE is the direct RL analogue.
Matches Ohio part b, retelling stories while maintaining meaning and logical order; genre-neutral Response strand applied here to literary retelling.
Both center on describing the main character, though Ohio frames it as response to events and TX as internal/external traits.
Supplies the 'major events and challenges' side of Ohio's standard via plot's main events, conflict, and resolution, but does not require tying character response to them.
Directly matches Ohio's 'repeated lines' element — explaining the use of repetition; genre-neutral Author's craft strand.
Rhyme scheme and sound devices in poems is the closest match to Ohio's beats/alliteration/rhyme focus; cross-grade +1 (TX grade 3, no grade-2 sound-device SE exists).
Both require describing overall story structure; TX names main events/conflict/resolution where Ohio names beginning and ending.
Identical skill — identifying first- vs third-person narration; genre-neutral Author's craft strand but applied to narrators, so it serves RL.

Both involve print/graphic features contributing to meaning, but TX frames it as author's purpose rather than using illustrations to understand character/setting/plot; genre-neutral, shared with RI.2.7.
Matches Ohio's explicit text-to-self and text-to-text connection clause; genre-neutral Comprehension strand (TX has no text-complexity-band standard).
Describing personal connections to sources covers Ohio's text-to-self/prior-experience clause; genre-neutral Response strand.
Both require grounding answers explicitly in text evidence; genre-neutral Response strand, shared with RI.3.1.
Covers the question-generation half of Ohio's ask-and-answer standard; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, shared with RI.3.1.
Both require inferring/determining a theme of a literary work; TX literary-elements SE is the direct RL analogue.
Matches Ohio part b, retelling stories in ways that maintain meaning and logical order; genre-neutral Response strand applied to literary retelling.
Both require characterization work at grade 3, though Ohio emphasizes traits/motivations and TX emphasizes major/minor character relationships.
Covers Ohio's requirement that character actions contribute to the sequence of events, via analyzing plot sequence, conflict, and resolution.
Shared skill is distinguishing literal from figurative/nonliteral language in a text; TX adds an author's-purpose frame Ohio does not require; genre-neutral Author's craft strand.
Matches Ohio's requirement to use structural terms such as stanza when referring to parts of poems.
Covers Ohio's drama terms (acts/scenes) but TX asks students to discuss drama elements rather than describe how successive parts build on earlier sections.
Identical skill — identifying first- vs third-person point of view; genre-neutral Author's craft strand applied to narration.
Both require explaining how graphic/print features contribute to what a text conveys; genre-neutral Author's craft strand, shared with RI.3.7.
Only TX SE in range demanding compare/contrast across texts, but it is genre-neutral and written-response framed, not the literary theme/setting/plot comparison Ohio names; cross-grade +1.
Matches Ohio's explicit text-to-self and text-to-text connection clause; genre-neutral Comprehension strand (TX has no text-complexity-band standard).
Describing personal connections to sources covers Ohio's prior-knowledge/text-to-self clause; genre-neutral Response strand.
Covers the 'ask' half of Ohio's ask-and-answer routine; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, same SE also serves RL.2.1.
Covers answering from the text with evidence about key details; genre-neutral Response strand, same SE also serves RL.2.1.
TX 2.9D(i) requires recognizing the central idea and supporting evidence of informational text, matching Ohio's main topic / paragraph focus.
Evaluating details to determine key ideas is the same main-idea operation; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.

TX 2.9D(iii) covers informational organizational patterns such as chronological order and explicit cause and effect, matching Ohio's connections among events/ideas/steps; same SE also carries RI.2.2 and RI.2.5 (TX collapses ~all RI structure into one SE).
TX 2.9D(ii) requires using features and graphics to locate and gain information, matching Ohio's use of text features to locate key facts efficiently.
Both require identifying the author's purpose for writing the text; genre-neutral Author's purpose strand.
Both involve graphic features clarifying a text, but TX frames it as author's purpose rather than explaining how an image contributes to the text; genre-neutral, shared with RL.2.7.
TX persuasive-text SE (what the author wants the reader to think/do, fact vs. opinion) is the grade-2 argument analogue to Ohio's author's main points and supporting reasons.
Both require grounding answers explicitly in text evidence; genre-neutral Response strand, same SE also serves RL.3.1.
Covers the question-generation half of Ohio's ask-and-answer standard; genre-neutral Comprehension strand, same SE also serves RL.3.1.
TX 3.9D(i) requires recognizing the central idea with supporting evidence in informational text, matching Ohio's main idea plus supporting key details.
Evaluating details to determine key ideas is the same main-idea-from-details operation; genre-neutral Comprehension strand.

TX 3.9D(iii) covers informational organizational patterns such as cause and effect, matching Ohio's time/sequence/cause-effect relationships among events, ideas, or steps; same SE also carries RI.3.2 and RI.3.5.
TX 3.9D(ii) covers informational features (sections, tables, graphs, timelines, bold font) to support understanding, though Ohio additionally requires search tools such as key words and hyperlinks.
Both require explaining how graphic/print features contribute to understanding of the text; genre-neutral Author's craft strand, shared with RL.3.7.
TX argumentative-text SE (identify the claim, distinguish fact from opinion) is the grade-3 analogue to Ohio's author's points, though it stops short of evidence-to-point relationships.
TX 4.9E(ii) 'explaining how the author has used facts for an argument' is the closest match to Ohio's evidence-to-point relationship; cross-grade +1, as grade 3 has no equivalent.
Only TX SE in range demanding compare/contrast of ideas across sources, but it is genre-neutral and written-response framed rather than an informational points/details comparison; cross-grade +1.

unmatched.why: TEKS grades 2-3 have no cross-text comparison SE; the only candidate (4.7B, compare/contrast ideas across sources) is two grades away from grade 2, outside the ±1 window.

Determining meaning of grade-level topic/subject words lives in the TEKS Vocabulary strand (2.3A-D), which is not among the candidate reading strands; no Comprehension/Response/genre SE demands word-meaning determination.
TEKS grades 2-3 have no SE requiring comparison of points across two texts on the same topic; 4.7B is two grades from grade 2, outside the ±1 window.
TEKS has no text-complexity-band proficiency standard, and unlike RL.2.10 this standard carries no connections clause to hook onto the genre-neutral Comprehension strand.
Academic and domain-specific vocabulary determination lives in the TEKS Vocabulary strand (3.3A-D), which is outside the candidate reading strands.

TEKS has no SE requiring students to distinguish their own perspective from the author's; 3.10A only asks students to explain the author's purpose and message, which is half the task and omits the distinguishing operation.
TEKS has no text-complexity-band proficiency standard, and unlike RL.3.10 this standard carries no connections clause to hook onto the genre-neutral Comprehension strand.
